sentences.text: Yes.
And I have been in Haganah since, you know, till Haganah became the Israeli army.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I graduated Mikveh Israel.
I was sent by the Haganah to the Dead Sea, which was I, which was I was in charge of the security from the Haganah to Dead Sea.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was there about year and a half.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was kind of difficult, very hot climate.
But we did a lot of work there.
I worked in the metal field in the daytime.
At night, security, you know, even when we worked with security also.
And, and I finished my term for a year and a  half.
Then I moved to Tel Aviv, which I lived with my sister [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and my younger brother.
My sister's husband remained in Russia.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he joined her about a year and a half later.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And, naturally, I continued to be member of the Haganah, active.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I worked in Tel Aviv.
No, very seldom-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I would say about a year and a half, I didn't hear from them.
Here and there, we used to get a letter.
They were afraid to write.
Till they end up in Germany in Eschwege camp, which they concentrate all the Jews, and then at the site where they want to move, where they want to settle.
Yes.
I, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] being in Tehran, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I succeeded to get a little job in, in the Jewish Agency.
And I became very friendly with the wife of the foreign minister, which was Mrs.
Herzog. ?] And, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and I found out the address of the camp.
And I was sending them packages.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then, being in Israel, I reconnected with the wife, foreign minister, trying to bring them into Israel.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I had all the papers ready.
And I send them out.
But they changed their mind.
They end up in the United States.
They came to the United States, , like '46, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the whole family except my younger brother, myself, and my oldest sister and her son.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I, I lived in Israel, you know, in Tel Aviv.
I worked.
I, I worked in a metal field.
Matter of fact, I was building ice factories.
And I was building oil factories, how to make oil from olive, olives, you know, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] til in the beginning of 1947.
And then I was mobilized by the Haganah full-time.
And we were sent out to different camps, exercise, getting ready, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] May, 1947, when [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Israel became a nation.
The night, can never forget, we arrived from about 17 days exercise, you know, field exercise.
And we arrived to Tel Aviv.
It was big celebration.
But we were so tired, we couldn't wait to go home and get-- to go to sleep.
And the orders were that, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that the following day, 5:00 in the morning, we have to get up and keep moving.
And they took us to Negev.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was already-- we were in the army.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was in Negev.
Then I was sent to an officers' school in Netanya.
And I became an officer.

And I was assigned to Hativat Givati, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which was one of the brigade which was known of the best brigades in Israel, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] which we lost about 350 soldiers during the war.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was in Givati till I was wounded.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And first time, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and this was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the big battle when the Arab nation's neighbors decided to attack Israel.
And our brigade was in Negev, which was the, the hottest battlefields in Israel at the time.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was an assistant commander [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] near a bridge which was connecting Egyptian side with Israeli side.
And they decided to attack our positions, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which we were there, about 180 soldiers.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they were advancing with tanks, full battlefield dress, and you know, planes.
We digged in.
And we fought them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We lost a lot of people, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] including our commander got wounded very hard.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we connected with the headquarters.
And he connected with the headquarters and, and described the battle.
And, and he asked who takes over the command.
And headquarters assigned me.
And I took over the command.
And we succeeded to push back the Egyptian army.
Then they got it through the radio that I got wounded, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which I, I didn't.
I got wounded, and I got killed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but it wasn't true.
But we fought.
We managed to push them back.
And we got reinforcement.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I can say proudly that this was the decisive battle.
In other words, we didn't have tens of thousands of soldiers, very hard.
We must have lost 60%, killed.
But then we got reinforcement.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we succeeded.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In 1992 was a reunion in Israel about Hativat Givati [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and the Negev, being there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A man jumped on me, crying, and hugging me.
And he said, Dov, I don't believe it's you.
He says, for the last forty years, I was thinking about it.
I know you were killed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was quite a moment.
But, anyway, then we were transferred to another camp.
And I would say for about three weeks, we used to go out every night to a battle to attack.
And normally, under normal circumstances, soldiers allowed to go out to a battle every third day.
One day, he goes to a battle, two days, he rests.
We used to go every, every day.
It was hard.
We used to lose a lot of people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my main fronts were the Negev.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I believe I was a pretty good commander.
And soldiers used to fight to be in my unit.
Because I-- see normally, the officers are in back of the unit attacking.
I always used to be always the first one.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one of the decisive battles was Hill 105.
Hill 105, there were, if you-- you know where Negba is?
Negba was surrounded with hill.
So Negba was taken over once by Egyptian army.
But then they were retreated.
And I came back from an action.
We were in a English camp which they left, after they left, you know, Israel.
And I was assigned to attack that hill.
And I had 120 people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the hill was like a 45 degree hill.
We had to attack from the bottom and attack the bunkers of the Egyptian army.
And it was, like, 12:30 in the morning.
And we were attacking the hill, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] different direction.
I had the center.
And I was advancing very quietly, which was to be strictly gain surprise.
And I would say we came within a distance of about 30 meters from the center of the main bunkers.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they noticed us, noticed us by shooting those night rockets, you know?
The minute they noticed that, they opened fire in different direction, heavy fire.
And I lost a lot of people, a lot of people, including I got hit with a bullet, my shoulder.
But I had movements.
And I was a very good hand grenade thrower.
We used to go-- I used to believe, you know, I used to have like 10-12 grenade hanging.
And I pull out a hand grenade.
And I make a direct hit to the bunker, which I killed 13 soldiers.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When the fire quiet down, we were advancing.
In the same time, we had a special jeep unit commando.
We used to call them [HEBREW SPEECH].
This was the reinforcement, which they came in from the side.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We took over the hill, lot of killed Egyptian.
And we opened up the gate to the Negba.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was wounded.
And then my hand started to swell up.
They took me down from the hill to the main road.
And I was laying there, you know, about an hour, hour and a half, til they pick me up.
And they took me to [INAUDIBLE].
But again, they didn't take me to the hospital-- to a special station.
And then they brought me over to, to the hospital, hospital [INAUDIBLE].
Then my hand swelled up black completely.
And they decided to amputate my hand.
But I was so aware.
I said, I'd rather die instead of take off my hand.
They say, must do it.
And I said, do anything you can.
But don't take off my hand.
And there were four men, nurses, holding me, you know?
They opened up.
They're trying to find the bullet to clean me up and everything.
But I succeeded.
They didn't take off.
They just cleaned me out and brought me to, you know, brought me to the bed.
And I slept for three and a half days.
But headlines in the paper was that I was killed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [?
And being ?] that hospital, I had two girlfriends.
They found out I was not killed in that hospital.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was the fifth day I was in the hospital.
And I heard that Hativat Givati [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] is going to attack Ashdod.
You know the port Ashdod, the big city?
By that time, it was a small city.
When I heard, I got dressed.
And I disappeared from the hospital.
And I joined the attack.
And this is the book, [INAUDIBLE], which they write about it.
Actually, when I came back, I went back to the hospital.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
I also participate in attacking Iraq Suwaydan, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] which was the fortress of the police in the area, which was built by the English.
And the commander of the police was [?
Naser. ?] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I was stationed.
As a matter of fact, I was in charge in Negba.
Because Negba was surrounded by the Egyptian and was under constant bombardment, you know, from the air and, and the mountains.
And naturally, we had bunkers all around.
You know, you couldn't walk, just out of the bunkers.
And I remembered that I was in a bunker facing Hill 69, which was a very dangerous hill.
And we were observing the hill, the movement.
Suddenly, I noticed that the command car arrived on that hill.
And they're getting out of the command.
And they were walking.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
I was a, a very good shooter.
And we had that time the Czechoslovakian machine guns.
Used to call them MG, you know?
And I aimed.
And I fired from the machine gun.
And I noticed, you know, listened that I hit it.
I made a good hit.
Maybe some ambulance came, you know?
And then I participated in attacking the fortress, which, till today, I cannot figure out why infantry attack that fortress.
Was one of the strongest, you know, spots from the Egyptian army, especially when [?
Naser ?] was the commander.
We surrounded it 360 degree, firing on them 24 hours.
We couldn't do a thing.
Almost every night, we were attacking.
We lost over 300 boys.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
And when I was in Israel to the reunion, the person who organized it, who was the commander at that time-- and we were very close, you know?
I contribute money for that purpose, many others.
And he spoke for about two hours to us, me and my closest friends, trying to figure out what right, what purpose, did we have to surround it, to attack so many times, to lose so many young boys.
But finally, we took it over.
We took it over to many months by sending a truck robot, without anybody, bring it to a certain spot and just, right by itself, loaded with dynamite.
And we hit the wall.
I would say the wall was about six feet wide, steel and everything.
But we succeeded in open up an opening.
And we took it over.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Was hard-- lost a lot of friends, very close friends.
But you know, life has to go on.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Also, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was a hill right next to that fortress, which was, I would say, a quarter of a mile square, you know, round hill, which they, sit there for over a month and thinking, we're trying to attack.
And we didn't succeed.
Until finally, we attacked it once we had-- I would say the Israeli army had three tanks, three Sherman tanks that we stole it from the English army.
And we attacked.
And that's how we took it over.
This was 1947 until 1949.
When the war end, I still worked for the army.
And at an office.
And soldiers who used to be in my unit, they used to come to me [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to help them to get settled, which I used to help them.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And one very memorable, not pleasant, you know, action was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was 1948.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] If you have been in Israel, the road from Tel Aviv to Jerusalem, in the middle of the road when you travel, you have remains of trucks, which they used to build a cabin from metal to prevent bullets.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Jerusalem was surrendered.
And the Jordanian army did not allow to bring in food.
They didn't have no water, no food.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the order was at any price to open up the way to Jerusalem.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They did build a side road.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But they wanted to open up the main road, the way you'd go from Tel Aviv to Jerusalem, where those trucks are-- the remains of trucks are there-- to get in and break the line.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The assignment they gave me was I got nine trucks with 72 soldiers.
Our equipment was machine guns and rifles.
We were stationed in Gaza, Kibbutz Gaza, right not far from there.
And I got the ten-- nine trucks.
And we were advancing.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But at the same time, supposedly, the English army was leaving those fortresses, the police stations.
While I was advancing on the road, there were English tanks cruising around the area, back and forth, which wasn't such a pleasant feeling.
And we felt that they were, like, on the Jordanian side.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When I advanced towards the main road, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which I was approaching [INAUDIBLE]-- this was the name of the area-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] which we had also on the left side of the road was a full brigade, volunteers from the United States, which they were digged in to cover us.
But we were advancing to [INAUDIBLE] in my truck with the freight.
